request.question: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?

title: A.L. "Doodle" Owens
I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again
I Never Once Stopped Loving You (song)
Wine Colored Roses (song)
All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)
The Right Left Hand
Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way
George Jones
Fourteen Minutes Old
sentences: Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.

He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.
In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".
"I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.
"(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in October 1969 as the first single from the album "Just Plain Charley". "
(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" was Charley Pride's second number one on the country charts.
The single stayed at number one for three weeks and spent a total of 15 weeks on the country charts.
"I Never Once Stopped Loving You" is a single by American country music artist Connie Smith.
Released in March 1970, the song reached #5 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
An album of the same name was released in September 1970 that included the song.
In addition, "I Never Once Stopped Loving You" also peaked at #17 on the Canadian "RPM" Country Tracks chart.
"Wine Colored Roses" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in September 1986 as the first single and title track from Jones' album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 10 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song tells the story of a man who receives a letter from a caring ex-lover who asks him if he still drinks.
Unable to overcome his own shame to speak with her on the telephone, he sends her a dozen wine colored roses as a symbol that he still drinks.

The song became part of Jones' live show at the time, and when he would sing the line, "She asked if I had quit drinkin'," audiences would often cheer, mindful of how George had gotten sober himself in 1984.

In his essay for the liner notes to the 1994 Sony compilation "The Essential George Jones: The Spirit of Country", Rich Kienzle states, "If there were any doubters, 'Wine Colored Roses' proved Jones was a timeless superstar, even without stimulants."
"All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.
It was released in June 1969 as the first single from the album "The Best of Charley Pride".
"The Right Left Hand" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in January 1987 as the second single from his album "Wine Colored Roses".
The song peaked at number 8 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.
The song is about marriage; the narrator rejoices at putting "a golden band on the right left hand" after several failed marriages.
Many interpreted the song as George's tribute to his fourth wife Nancy, whom he credited with saving his life and career.
Jones would not see the Top 10 again until late 1988.
"Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way" is a song written by Dennis Knutson and A.L. "Doodle" Owens and recorded by American country music artist George Jones.
It was released in April 1986 as the third single from his album "Who's Gonna Fill Their Shoes".
The song peaked at number 9 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles chart.

The song is very much in the tradition of George's previous hits "Still Doin' Time" and "If Drinkin' Don't Kill Me (Her Memory Will)" in that it is a hard "drinking song," but it's also a "cheatin'" song, with the narrator suspicious that "Somebody keeps paying my bar tab but the bartender won't tell me who."
Jones delivers a soulful, bluesy vocal over a stone country arrangement with a prominent, shimmering acoustic guitar.
The singer, who had been a notorious drinker, was sober by this time thanks to his wife Nancy, who had gotten his career back on track after years of mismanagement and missed concert dates.
George Glenn Jones (September 12, 1931 – April 26, 2013) was an American musician, singer and songwriter.
He achieved international fame for his long list of hit records, including his best known song "He Stopped Loving Her Today", as well as his distinctive voice and phrasing.
For the last twenty years of his life, Jones was frequently referred to as the greatest living country singer.

Country music scholar Bill C. Malone writes, "For the two or three minutes consumed by a song, Jones immerses himself so completely in its lyrics, and in the mood it conveys, that the listener can scarcely avoid becoming similarly involved."
Waylon Jennings expressed a similar opinion in his song "It's Alright": "If we all could sound like we wanted to, we'd all sound like George Jones."
The shape of his nose and facial features earned Jones the nickname "The Possum."
"Fourteen Minutes Old" is a song written by A.L. "Doodle" Owens and Dennis Knutson, and recorded by American country music artist Doug Stone.
It was released in June 1990 as the second single from his self titled debut album.
It peaked at number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart and number 5 on The Canadian RPM Tracks chart.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Who did A.L. "Doodle" Owens work with to write many songs for the singer of "He Stopped Loving Her Today"?
hop1_docs: A.L. "Doodle" Owens | Arthur Leo "Doodle" Owens (November 28, 1930 – October 4, 1999) was an American country music songwriter and singer.  He had a long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier, with whom he wrote "All I Have to Offer You (Is Me)" (1969), "(I'm So) Afraid of Losing You Again" (1969), "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" (1970) and "Then Who Am I" (1974), all number-one country hits for Charlie Pride.  In the 1980s, Owens wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones and other artists.

I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me | "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" is a song written by Dallas Frazier and A.L. "Doodle" Owens, and recorded by American country music artist Charley Pride.  It was released in September 1970 as the second single from the album "From Me to You".  "I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me" was Charley Pride's fifth number one on the country charts.  The single stayed at number one for two weeks and spent a total of fifteen weeks on the country charts.

Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song) | "Falling in Love Again" is a 1977 song recorded by singer Marvin Gaye and issued on his 1978 album, "Here, My Dear" album.  The song was another track on the personal album that did not discuss the demise of his first marriage.  Instead of Anna Gaye, the song talked of the other woman in Marvin's life.  Described in "You Can Leave, but It's Going to Cost You" as "that young girl", she was Janis Hunter, whom Gaye had married.  In a solemn but still certain tone, he wanted to be sure that this time his love for Janis will be what he had always wanted.  But as irony would have it, by the time of the album's release, Marvin and Janis' relationship was failing.  By the end of the album's promotion, Janis had split from the singer after nearly two years as a married couple.  They eventually divorced in February 1981.  This song was the last song on "Here, My Dear" with a reprise from the album's "theme song", "When Did You Stop Loving Me, When Did I Stop Loving You", playing soon afterwards putting the album to a close for good.

He Stopped Loving Her Today | "He Stopped Loving Her Today" is a song recorded by American country music artist George Jones.  It has been named in several surveys as the greatest country song of all time.  It was released in April 1980 as the lead single from the album "I Am What I Am".  The song was Jones's first solo No. 1 single in six years.  The melancholy song was written by Bobby Braddock and Curly Putman.  The week after Jones' death the song re-entered the Hot Country Songs chart at No. 21.  As of November 13, 2013, the single has sold 521,000 copies in the United States.  Since 2008 it has been preserved by the Library of Congress into the National Recording Registry.

Put It in a Love Song | "Put It in a Love Song" is a song by American singer Alicia Keys.  The song, written and produced by Keys and Swizz Beatz, features guest vocals by American R&B singer Beyoncé Knowles.  It was originally sent to radio on January 19, 2010 by J Records, as the fourth single from Keys' fourth studio album, "The Element of Freedom" (2009).  However, the full single release was postponed, so that "Un-Thinkable (I'm Ready)" could be pushed forward as the fourth single.  The R&B and soul song, talks about a girl who just wants her lover to write her a song and prove to her he's worth her time.

The Yankee Doodle Boy | "The Yankee Doodle Boy", also well known as "(I'm a) Yankee Doodle Dandy" is a patriotic song from the Broadway musical "Little Johnny Jones" written by George M. Cohan.  The play opened at the Liberty Theater on November 7, 1904.  The play concerns the trials and tribulations of a fictional American jockey, Johnny Jones (based on the real life jockey Tod Sloan), who rides a horse named "Yankee Doodle" in the English Derby.  Cohan incorporates snippets of several popular traditional American songs into his lyrics of this song, as he often did with his songs.  The song was performed by James Cagney in the 1942 film "Yankee Doodle Dandy", in which he played Cohan.

The Bird (Jerry Reed song) | "The Bird" is a song recorded by American country music singer Jerry Reed.  Written by Hal Coleman and Barry Etris, this novelty song contains impressions of Willie Nelson's "Whiskey River" and "On the Road Again;" and George Jones' "He Stopped Loving Her Today."  It was released in October 1982 as the lead single from the album, "The Bird".  The song peaked at No. 2 on the "Billboard magazine" Hot Country Singles chart just before Christmas.
hop1_titles: I Can't Believe That You've Stopped Loving Me
Falling in Love Again (Marvin Gaye song)
Put It in a Love Song

1) Evidence found:
- A.L. "Doodle" Owens page (passage [1]): quotes "wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones" and notes "long songwriting partnership with Dallas Frazier" (both tokens from passage [1]).
- "He Stopped Loving Her Today" page (passage [4]): identifies the song as "recorded by American country music artist George Jones" (passage [4]).

2) Ambiguities:
- Two collaborator names appear in the passages: "Dennis Knutson" (explicitly linked to George Jones in passage [1]) and "Dallas Frazier" (partner with Owens in passage [1], but associated in passages [1]/[2] with Charley Pride). Do not assume which collaborator the question intends without checking credits.
- A separate dedicated page for Dennis Knutson is not included among the provided passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as given in the passages): Dennis Knutson.
- Missing fact needed to answer the question: authoritative confirmation that A.L. "Doodle" Owens and Dennis Knutson co‑wrote "many songs" for George Jones — specifically, the songwriting credits or list of George Jones recordings co‑credited to Owens and Knutson (song titles and/or dates).

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open "A.L. 'Doodle' Owens" page: check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Songwriting" sections for mentions of Dennis Knutson and lists of co‑written songs for George Jones.
  - Search keywords: "A.L. Doodle Owens Dennis Knutson George Jones songwriting credits".
- Open "Dennis Knutson" page (or disambiguation): check lede and songwriting credits, and any "Works" or "Selected compositions" listing songs written for George Jones.
  - Search keywords: "Dennis Knutson songs George Jones Owens".
- Open "George Jones" page and relevant album pages (e.g., 1980s albums, 'I Am What I Am', discography): check song lists and liner credits for songs credited to Owens and Knutson.
  - Sections to check: lede/infobox, Discography, Albums (1980s), and song/track listing credits.
- Optionally: fetch dedicated song pages for notable George Jones tracks from the 1980s to see songwriting credits.

Do not provide the final answer yet — fetch the pages/sections above to verify songwriting credits and list the specific co‑written songs.

It was released in June 1990 as the second single from his self titled debut album.  It peaked at number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart and number 5 on The Canadian RPM Tracks chart.
J. Knutson | James Jay "J." Knutson is a Canadian folk singer-songwriter.  He is normally credited with the initial J.

Little Johnny Jones | Little Johnny Jones is a musical by George M. Cohan.  The show introduced Cohan's tunes "Give My Regards to Broadway" and "The Yankee Doodle Boy."  The "Yankee Doodle" character was inspired by real-life Hall of Fame jockey Tod Sloan.
hop2_titles: Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way
response.summary_2: wrote many songs with fellow songwriter Dennis Knutson for George Jones. Dennis Knutson co‑wrote George Jones recordings including "Wine Colored Roses", "The Right Left Hand", and "Somebody Wants Me Out of the Way".
